_scenario: Infant 2 months — Motor Development priority. Actual LLM content will vary; structure must match exactly.
child_summary: This 7-day plan focuses on strengthening Motor Development for your 2-month-old in MONITOR mode. Communication and Social Engagement are on-track. At least 2 of 3 daily activities target motor skills, progressing gently from Day 2 to Day 7.

activities.title: Tummy Time Reach
activities.description: Place baby on tummy and hold a bright toy just out of reach. Do 2-3 sessions a day for 40 seconds.
steps: Place baby on tummy and hold a bright toy just out of reach. Do 2-3 sessions a day for 40 seconds.
activities.tip: Observe how your child responds and note any new reactions today.

activities.title: Face Talk
activities.description: Hold baby 30 cm away and make exaggerated facial expressions. Wait for baby to respond.
steps: Hold baby 30 cm away and make exaggerated facial expressions. Wait for baby to respond.
activities.tip: Observe how your child responds and note any new reactions today.

activities.title: Wobble Balance
activities.description: Tilt baby gently side to side while holding them safely. Helps them feel balance.
steps: Tilt baby gently side to side while holding them safely. Helps them feel balance.
activities.tip: Observe how your child responds and note any new reactions today.

activities.title: Gentle Limb Swings
activities.description: Gently move baby's arms and legs in small circles to promote body awareness and muscle engagement.
steps: Lay baby on their back on a soft, flat surface.
Gently hold one arm and move it in a slow circle 5 times.
Repeat with the other arm.
Gently hold each leg and move in slow circles 5 times each.
activities.tip: Moving your baby's limbs helps them explore their body's range of motion and builds early muscle memory.

activities.title: Tummy Time with Mirror
activities.description: Place a small unbreakable mirror in front of baby during tummy time to motivate head lifting.
steps: Lay baby on their tummy on a firm mat.
Place a small unbreakable mirror 20 cm in front of baby's face.
Wait quietly — baby will naturally lift their head to look.
Praise any head lift with a warm smile.
activities.tip: Lifting the head against gravity is the key early step for neck and shoulder strength.

activities.title: Sound & Turn
activities.description: Make gentle sounds from different sides of baby's head to encourage them to turn toward the source.
steps: Lay baby on their back.
Hold a soft squeaky toy 30 cm to their left and give one gentle squeeze.
Wait 5 seconds for baby to turn their head.
Repeat on the right side. Do 3 rounds per side.
activities.tip: Turning toward sound trains both communication awareness and neck rotation at the same time.

activities.title: Chest-Lift Tummy Time
activities.description: Roll a small towel under baby's chest during tummy time to help them hold their head higher for longer.
steps: Roll a small hand towel into a tight log shape.
Lay baby on tummy and slide the towel under their chest so shoulders are slightly raised.
Place a colorful toy 25 cm in front at eye level.
Encourage them to look up at the toy for 3 seconds, rest, repeat.
activities.tip: The towel reduces gravitational load so baby can practice head lifting longer, building endurance faster.

activities.title: Arm Pull-Up
activities.description: Gently hold baby's wrists and slowly pull toward sitting to engage their core and neck.
steps: Lay baby on their back on a firm surface.
Gently wrap your fingers around both wrists.
Slowly pull their arms upward, lifting their upper body just 5 cm off the mat.
Hold 2 seconds, lower smoothly. Repeat 3 times.
activities.tip: Assisted pull-ups activate core muscles and help baby begin to understand their own body weight.

activities.title: Face Chat
activities.description: Hold baby face-to-face and make slow expressions — smile, surprise — then wait for imitation.
steps: Hold baby at arm's length, faces 25 cm apart.
Make a big smile, hold 3 seconds.
Switch to a wide surprised face, hold 3 seconds.
Wait silently 5 seconds after each — give baby time to respond.
activities.tip: Facial imitation is the earliest form of social learning — pauses teach baby that communication is two-way.

activities.title: Forearm Weight Bear
activities.description: Position baby on tummy with elbows directly under shoulders to bear weight on forearms.
steps: Lay baby on tummy on a firm mat.
Gently position elbows directly below shoulders, forearms flat.
Place hands lightly on upper back for encouragement — do not push.
Hold a toy at eye level 20 cm away for 5 seconds, then lower for rest.
activities.tip: Forearm weight-bearing builds the shoulder stability needed for rolling and sitting.

activities.title: Side-Lying Reach
activities.description: Roll baby onto their side and place a toy just out of reach so they practice reaching across their midline.
steps: Roll baby onto their right side, supported by a rolled blanket at their back.
Hold a bright toy 15 cm in front of their left hand.
Wait 5 seconds for baby to reach across.
Switch sides and repeat.
activities.tip: Crossing the midline trains the core rotation muscles that baby needs for rolling.

activities.title: Name Game
activities.description: Say baby's name in different tones and watch for them to still or look toward your voice.
steps: Sit 30 cm from baby while they lie on their back.
Say their name once in a soft whisper, wait 5 seconds.
Say their name in a sing-song rhythm, wait 5 seconds.
Watch for stillness, eye movement, or head turn.
activities.tip: Recognizing their own name is one of the first language milestones.

activities.title: Guided Roll — Side to Back
activities.description: From a side-lying position, gently guide baby's hip so they complete a roll onto their back.
steps: Roll baby onto their right side and let them settle 3 seconds.
Place your left hand lightly on their right hip.
Very gently nudge the hip forward so baby rolls onto their back.
Celebrate and repeat on the left side.
activities.tip: Facilitated rolling teaches the movement pattern before baby has the strength to do it alone.

activities.title: Grasp & Shake
activities.description: Press a lightweight rattle into baby's palm so they reflexively grasp it, then gently shake their hand so they hear the sound.
steps: Hold a lightweight rattle near baby's open palm.
Touch the handle to their palm — they will close fingers around it.
Gently move their hand up and down so the rattle sounds.
Let them hold 10 seconds, then repeat.
activities.tip: Cause and effect — I shake, I hear — is one of the earliest cognitive-motor links babies develop.

activities.title: Peek-a-Boo Cloth
activities.description: Cover your face with a thin muslin cloth and pull it away with a 'boo!' to spark early object awareness.
steps: Hold baby in a supported recline.
Hold a thin muslin cloth at chin level.
Raise it to cover your face, pause 2 seconds.
Pull it away quickly and say 'Boo!' with a big smile. Repeat 4 times.
activities.tip: Anticipating your reappearance is baby's first lesson in object permanence.

activities.title: Over-Thigh Tummy Time
activities.description: Drape baby over your thigh on their tummy so their head hangs slightly, encouraging lifting against gravity.
steps: Sit on the floor with legs stretched out.
Drape baby over your right thigh, tummy on your leg, head facing slightly down.
Support their hips with one hand.
Let baby lift their head against gravity. Hold a toy as incentive. Do 3 short lifts.
activities.tip: Changing the angle of tummy time works different muscle groups — variety builds strength faster.

activities.title: Hands-to-Mouth Guide
activities.description: Guide baby's hands toward their mouth so they begin to discover their own body intentionally.
steps: Lay baby on their back in a relaxed position.
Gently hold both wrists and slowly bring hands together above their chest.
Guide hands toward their mouth and let them touch their lips.
Release and watch if they try independently. Repeat 3 times.
activities.tip: Hands-to-mouth is a self-soothing milestone — baby is beginning to understand where their body ends.

activities.title: Copy My Sound
activities.description: Make simple vowel sounds — aah, ooh, eeh — pause, and wait for baby to vocalize back.
steps: Hold baby face-to-face at arm's length.
Make a clear 'aah' sound, hold 2 seconds, then stop.
Lean forward and wait silently 8 seconds.
If baby vocalizes — any sound counts — mirror it back. Try 3 different vowels.
activities.tip: These vowel exchanges are the foundation of turn-taking — baby is learning their voice gets a response.

activities.title: Solo Tummy Time Challenge
activities.description: Let baby attempt tummy time without your hand on their back — spot from close range to build confidence.
steps: Place baby on tummy on a firm mat with a mirror or toy in front.
Keep your hands 2 cm from their sides but do not touch.
If baby struggles after 10 seconds, place one finger on their back briefly, then remove.
Aim for 2 independent head lifts. Celebrate every attempt.
activities.tip: Reducing your support builds baby's proprioception — their sense of their own body in space.

activities.title: Full-Field Toy Tracking
activities.description: Move a brightly colored toy slowly across baby's full visual field — left to right, then top to bottom.
steps: Lay baby on their back in a calm, well-lit room.
Hold a red or yellow toy 25 cm from baby's face.
Move it slowly to the right over 5 seconds, then back to center.
Move it slowly downward over 5 seconds. Watch eyes track the full path.
activities.tip: Smooth visual tracking shows that the motor pathways between eyes and brain are maturing well.

activities.title: Second-Person Smile
activities.description: Hold baby facing outward so a second caregiver can approach slowly and earn a social smile.
steps: Hold baby facing outward, supported under arms and bottom.
Ask a second person to approach slowly from 1 meter away with a calm, smiling face.
Encourage the second person to call baby's name softly twice.
Watch for baby to still, make eye contact, and smile.
activities.tip: Expanding baby's social circle from one familiar face to two teaches them that multiple people are safe.

general_tips: Do tummy time on a firm surface only — a soft mattress reduces the resistance baby needs to build strength.
Watch for tired cues (turning away, fussing) and always end before baby hits their limit.
Narrate everything in a calm voice — this combines language exposure with every motor activity.
Consistency matters more than duration — 3 sessions of 40 seconds beats one session of 2 minutes.
Motor progress at this age is nonlinear — some days baby will do less. That is normal; keep going.
